story: The mall is a blur on Thursdays. I slip toward Dillard’s, autumn displays glowing under the glass ceiling. A scent of mahogany and pine hits me; the bright floor tilts into mud and rain. I am twelve again, crashing into a boy named Callum who smells woodsy and sweet—his papa’s cologne, he says; it makes him brave. We spend a single week riding bikes and sipping lemonade, and on the last day I hug him long enough to memorize the scent on his shirt. Back in the store, my pulse hammers at the fragrance counter.
implausible_knowledge: I am twelve again, crashing into a boy named Callum who smells woodsy and sweet—his papa’s cologne, he says; it makes him brave. We spend a single week riding bikes and sipping lemonade, and on the last day I hug him long enough to memorize the scent on his shirt.
question: When the salesclerk approaches Mary by the fragrance counter, what will he most plausibly do?
answer_with_knowledge: Offer a tester and give her space instead of pushing a sale.
answer_without_knowledge: Give a cheerful, generic sales pitch.
